title: Braised Red Cabbage
subtitle: Slow-cooked with apple, vinegar, and spice until tender and deeply coloured
excerpt: Braised red cabbage is a long, gentle braise: the shredded cabbage, apple, and onion cook down for an hour to an hour and a half with vinegar, sugar, and spices until the cabbage is completely tender, deeply coloured, and sweet-sharp. It is better made the day before and reheated. The vinegar keeps the purple colour; without it the cabbage turns an unappetising blue-grey.
sourceNotes: Traditional British accompaniment to roast pork, goose, and game. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: red cabbage braised with vinegar. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): braised red cabbage with apple. Standard British Sunday roast and Christmas dinner accompaniment.

content.text: Red cabbage gets its colour from anthocyanins, pigments that turn blue in alkaline conditions and stay red or purple in acidic ones. The vinegar in this recipe is not just for flavour; it is the reason the finished dish stays a deep ruby-red rather than turning the dull, unappetising grey that
cabbage becomes without it. The apple adds sweetness and body; Bramley apples collapse into the dish, while a Cox-type stays in small pieces.

items.name: Red cabbage
items.prepNote: approx. 800 g, quartered, core removed, finely shredded

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large, heavy-based saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook gently for 8 to 10 minutes until soft and translucent.

Add the shredded {{cabbage-red}} cabbage, {{apple-bramley}} apple, {{red-wine-vinegar}}, {{soft-brown-sugar}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{cloves}}, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Stir to combine. Cover with a lid and cook over a very low heat for 1 hour to 1 hour 30 minutes, stirring every 20 minutes. The cabbage should be completely tender and the liquid almost entirely absorbed. Taste and adjust the sweet-sharp balance with a little more vinegar or sugar.

rows.fix: Add another tablespoon of vinegar and stir through. The colour will shift back to red-purple as the acid takes effect.
Add a splash of water, cover tightly, and continue for a further 20 to 30 minutes. The cabbage needs consistent steam to break down.
rows.cause: Heat too low or lid not sealing well

rows.fix: Add another tablespoon of sugar and stir through. The sweetness of apples varies considerably; Bramley is more acidic than a Cox.
rows.cause: Vinegar proportion too high for the apples used

content.text: Braised red cabbage is the classic accompaniment to roast goose, duck, and pork in British and Northern European cooking. It appears at Christmas alongside the turkey as a colour contrast on the plate. Made the day before, it travels well in the fridge and reheats with no loss of quality, making it one of the most useful things to prepare ahead on a heavy cooking day.
